metadata.title: The Builder Who Said No

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Advaith Krishna Vasisht — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Advaith came to educational technology as an inheritance, not as a career move. Rather than patch a broken system, he rebuilt it around large language models. A real-time content verification engine fact-checks generated course material as it is made. Event-driven Lambda with auto-scaling inference cut course time 65% and costs 60%.

artifactLines: Advaith came to educational technology as an inheritance, not as a career move.
Rather than patch a broken system, he rebuilt it around large language models.
A real-time content verification engine fact-checks generated course material as it is made.
Event-driven Lambda with auto-scaling inference cut course time 65% and costs 60%.

beats.narration_text: What strikes me is that Advaith treated educational technology as an inheritance rather than a career move, and instead of patching a broken system he rebuilt it, cutting course-creation time 65% and infrastructure costs 60% with event-driven AWS Lambda and a fact-checking verification engine. I want to understand his architecture. Walk me through: (1) how auto-scaling inference on Lambda produces that 60% cost drop versus always-on servers; (2) what the content verification engine checks in real time; (3) which single decision drove the 65% authoring speedup.

props.command: What strikes me is that Advaith treated educational technology as an inheritance rather than a career move, and instead of patching a broken system he rebuilt it, cutting course-creation time 65% and infrastructure costs 60% with event-driven AWS Lambda and a fact-checking verification engine. I want to understand his architecture. Walk me through: (1) how auto-scaling inference on Lambda produces that 60% cost drop versus always-on servers; (2) what the content verification engine checks in real time; (3) which single decision drove the 65% authoring speedup.
output: Architectural decisions — the kind that close doors, or open them
Advaith did not arrive at educational technology as a career move
His parents and grandparents are university professors — physics,

beats.narration_text: He integrated large language models and engineered a content verification engine with real-time fact-checking. He implemented event-driven workflows on AWS Lambda with auto-scaling inference that reduced infrastructure costs by 60%.

beats.narration_text: Written by Advaith Krishna Vasisht — Humanitarians AI Fellow. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — Take the central idea from 'The Builder Who Said No' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then… That's the prompt that actually tests whether you got it. Run it and see where it surprises you.

props.command: Take the central idea from 'The Builder Who Said No' and give me the three most actionable takeaways — one sentence each. Then name the failure mode I'm most likely to hit.
